content:
# Disagreement Log — Leviticus 2

**Total disagreements: 15**

---

**v.1 — Subject of the offering: nefesh vs. "anyone"**
- EMET: "when a person [nefesh] brings near an offering [qorban] of grain offering [minchah] to the LORD"
- LOGOS: "when anyone brings a grain offering to the LORD"
- PNEUMA: "when anyone brings a grain offering to the LORD"

SOPHIA's ruling: LOGOS and PNEUMA agree on "anyone." EMET is correct that the Hebrew subject is נֶפֶשׁ (nefesh), a word with theological weight, and that the rabbinic tradition reads it deliberately. However, "a person" is the closest functional English equivalent for nefesh used as an indefinite subject, and the bracketed note in EMET is appropriate for textual commentary rather than the translation itself. SOPHIA adopts "anyone" from the LOGOS/PNEUMA consensus for readability, but adds a bracketed note "(nefesh — a soul, a person)" at first occurrence to honor EMET's observation. EMET's "brings near" (a more literal rendering of hiqriv) is a meaningful translational choice that LOGOS and PNEUMA abandon; SOPHIA retains "brings" as the simpler English while noting the Hebrew.

---

**v.1 — Frankincense application: "upon it" vs. "on it" vs. "on top of it"**
- EMET: "pour oil upon it and put frankincense [levonah] upon it"
- LOGOS: "pour oil on it and put frankincense on it"
- PNEUMA: "pour oil over it and lay frankincense on top of it"

SOPHIA's ruling: These are stylistic variants. "Pour oil over it" (PNEUMA) captures the pouring motion well; "lay frankincense on it" (PNEUMA) is natural English. LOGOS "put frankincense on it" is functionally identical. EMET's archaic "upon" is accurate but slightly stiff. SOPHIA adopts LOGOS's wording ("pour oil on it and put frankincense on it") as the clean middle register — concrete, not archaic, not colloquial.

---

**v.2 — Name form and family genitive: "sons of Aharon, the priests" vs. "sons of Aaron, the priests" vs. "Aaron's sons the priests"**
- EMET: "he shall bring it to the sons of Aharon, the priests"
- LOGOS: "he shall bring it to the sons of Aaron, the priests"
- PNEUMA: "bring it to Aaron's sons the priests"

SOPHIA's ruling: "Aaron" is the established English name form (project convention). "Sons of Aaron, the priests" (LOGOS) preserves the Hebrew construct phrase sons-of-Aaron more naturally than PNEUMA's possessive inversion. SOPHIA follows LOGOS.

---

**v.2 — The priest's taking: "his handful" vs. "a handful" vs. "a handful"**
- EMET: "shall take from it his handful [qomtso] of its fine flour and of its oil, together with all its frankincense"
- LOGOS: "shall take a handful of the fine flour and oil from it, along with all of its frankincense"
- PNEUMA: "shall take a handful of the fine flour and oil, together with all the frankincense"

SOPHIA's ruling: EMET's "his handful" is the more literal reading — the suffix on qomtso is third-person singular, i.e., "his handful," referring to a specific priestly measure. LOGOS and PNEUMA both render "a handful," which reads more naturally but loses the technical possessive. SOPHIA adopts "a handful" for English readability, following the LOGOS/PNEUMA majority, but notes the technical force of qomtso in Council Notes.

---

**v.2 — "Pleasing aroma" vs. "fragrant aroma"**
- EMET: "a pleasing aroma [re'ach nichoach] to the LORD"
- LOGOS: "a pleasing aroma to the LORD"
- PNEUMA: "a fragrant aroma to the LORD"

SOPHIA's ruling: Hebrew רֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ (re'ach nichoach) is a fixed cultic formula: re'ach = "aroma/smell," nichoach = "pleasing/soothing/restful." "Pleasing aroma" (EMET/LOGOS) captures both components — the sensory and the qualitative judgment. PNEUMA's "fragrant aroma" retains the sensory but loses the evaluative "pleasing/restful." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS: "a pleasing aroma to the LORD."

---

**v.3 — Priestly portion: "shall be for" vs. "shall belong to" vs. "belongs to"**
- EMET: "the remainder of the grain offering shall be for Aaron and for his sons — most holy from the fire offerings of the LORD"
- LOGOS: "what remains of the grain offering shall belong to Aaron and his sons; it is a most holy portion from the fire offerings of the LORD"
- PNEUMA: "What remains of the grain offering belongs to Aaron and his sons; it is most holy, taken from the LORD's fire-offerings"

SOPHIA's ruling: Three differences in this verse. (1) "Remainder/what remains": both capture ha-noteret; "what remains" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is more idiomatic. (2) "Shall be for/shall belong to/belongs to": the Hebrew yihyeh le- is a future-tense attribution; "shall belong to" (LOGOS) preserves the future/prescriptive force better than PNEUMA's simple present "belongs to." (3) "Most holy" vs "most holy portion": EMET and PNEUMA render qodesh qodashim as "most holy" (adjectival); LOGOS adds "portion" to make it a noun phrase. The Hebrew qodesh qodashim is in apposition to "what remains" — it is predicative ("it is most holy"), so EMET/PNEUMA's adjectival use is defensible, but LOGOS's "most holy portion" reads more clearly as designating a priestly food category. SOPHIA adopts LOGOS's full rendering: "what remains of the grain offering shall belong to Aaron and his sons; it is a most holy portion from the fire offerings of the LORD."

---

**v.4 — "Ring-cakes" vs. "loaves"**
- EMET: "unleavened ring-cakes [challot matsot] of fine flour mixed with oil, or unleavened wafers [riqquey matsot] smeared with oil"
- LOGOS: "unleavened loaves of fine flour mixed with oil, or unleavened wafers smeared with oil"
- PNEUMA: "unleavened loaves of fine flour mixed with oil, or unleavened wafers brushed with oil"

SOPHIA's ruling: Two sub-issues. (1) Challot: EMET renders "ring-cakes," LOGOS and PNEUMA render "loaves." The Hebrew challah denotes a round, thick cake — the cognate of modern challah bread. "Loaves" is generic; "ring-cakes" is more specific to the shape. However, "loaves" is the majority rendering in formal equivalence translations and avoids modern associations (challah = braided bread). SOPHIA adopts "loaves" following LOGOS and PNEUMA, with a note. (2) Smeared/brushed: EMET and LOGOS use "smeared with oil" for meshuchim; PNEUMA uses "brushed with oil." "Smeared" is the more precise rendering of the root m-sh-ch (anoint/spread), which is also the root of mashiach (anointed/messiah). SOPHIA retains "smeared" from EMET and LOGOS.

---

**v.5 — Word order and leaven phrasing**
- EMET: "it shall be fine flour unleavened, mixed with oil"
- LOGOS: "it shall be of fine flour, unleavened, mixed with oil"
- PNEUMA: "it shall be of fine flour mixed with oil and without leaven"

SOPHIA's ruling: PNEUMA reorders the elements, placing the leaven prohibition after the oil mixing, which reverses the Hebrew word order (solet bilti chametz belulah ba-shemen — "fine flour not-leavened mixed-with oil"). EMET and LOGOS preserve the Hebrew sequence. SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "it shall be of fine flour, unleavened, mixed with oil."

---

**v.8 — How the offering is presented: "one shall bring it near" vs. "it shall be presented" vs. "when it is presented"**
- EMET: "and one shall bring it near to the priest, and he shall bring it near to the altar"
- LOGOS: "And it shall be presented to the priest, and he shall bring it to the altar"
- PNEUMA: "and when it is presented to the priest, he shall carry it to the altar"

SOPHIA's ruling: The Hebrew ve-heviah...el ha-kohen ve-higriv el ha-mizbeach literally reads "and he brought it to the priest and he brought it near to the altar." EMET's "one shall bring it near" is a literal English rendering. LOGOS's passive "it shall be presented" follows the implied subject shift. PNEUMA's "when it is presented/he shall carry it" breaks the parallelism. SOPHIA adopts LOGOS for clarity: "And it shall be presented to the priest, and he shall bring it to the altar."

---

**v.9 — Verb for taking the memorial portion: "lift" vs. "lift out" vs. "take"**
- EMET: "the priest shall lift [ve-herim] from the grain offering its memorial portion"
- LOGOS: "the priest shall lift out from the grain offering its memorial portion"
- PNEUMA: "the priest shall take from the grain offering its memorial portion"

SOPHIA's ruling: Hebrew ve-herim (hif'il of rum, "to lift/raise") is the technical term for priestly removal of a portion from an offering. EMET's "lift" and LOGOS's "lift out" both preserve this root meaning. PNEUMA's "take" is generic and loses the technical character of the act — this is a lifting/elevating gesture, not a mere taking. SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: "the priest shall lift out from the grain offering its memorial portion," with "lift out" preferred over bare "lift" for clarity in English.

---

**v.11 — Sentence construction: positive framing vs. negative framing; "shall not burn" vs. "you must not burn"**
- EMET: "Every grain offering that you bring near to the LORD shall not be made with leaven, for any leaven and any honey you shall not burn from it as a fire offering to the LORD"
- LOGOS: "No grain offering that you bring to the LORD shall be made with leaven, for you shall burn no leaven and no honey as a fire offering to the LORD"
- PNEUMA: "No grain offering that you bring to the LORD may be made with leaven, for you must not burn any leaven or any honey as a fire-offering to the LORD"

SOPHIA's ruling: Three sub-issues. (1) Opening construction: EMET uses "Every...shall not" (positive subject + negative predicate); LOGOS and PNEUMA use "No grain offering...shall/may be made." The latter is crisper English. (2) Modal verb in clause two: LOGOS uses "you shall burn no leaven" (jussive/prescriptive force); PNEUMA uses "you must not burn" (stronger prohibition). The Hebrew is lo taqtiru — a simple imperfect with negation, read as a prescriptive prohibition. "You shall not burn" (following LOGOS/EMET) preserves the Torah's characteristic legislative register. (3) "Or" vs "and" between leaven and honey: PNEUMA uses "or" while EMET uses "and." Hebrew has "any leaven ve-any honey" with ve- (and/or). LOGOS renders "no leaven and no honey." Both "and" and "or" are defensible, but "and no honey" (LOGOS) follows the coordinate syntax more closely. SOPHIA follows LOGOS throughout this verse.

---

**v.12 — Word order and "bring near" vs "bring"**
- EMET: "As an offering of firstfruits [reishit] you may bring them near to the LORD, but they shall not go up upon the altar as a pleasing aroma"
- LOGOS: "As an offering of firstfruits you may bring them to the LORD, but they shall not go up on the altar for a pleasing aroma"
- PNEUMA: "You may bring them to the LORD as an offering of firstfruits, but they must not be offered on the altar as a pleasing aroma"

SOPHIA's ruling: (1) "As an offering of firstfruits" leading the clause (EMET/LOGOS) places the reishit in the emphatic fronted position as in the Hebrew. PNEUMA's back-placement weakens this. (2) PNEUMA's "they must not be offered on the altar" obscures the distinction between "being brought to the LORD" and "going up on the altar as a pleasing aroma" — the Hebrew has a specific verb for ascending the altar (ya'aleh) that distinguishes dedication from burning. EMET and LOGOS preserve "they shall not go up on the altar" more faithfully. SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "As an offering of firstfruits you may bring them to the LORD, but they shall not go up on the altar for a pleasing aroma."

---

**v.13 — Salt command structure: "you shall salt...with salt" vs. "season with salt" vs. "Season...with salt"**
- EMET: "every offering of your grain offering with salt you shall salt [titmalach], and you shall not let the salt of the covenant of your God be lacking [tashbit] from upon your grain offering — upon all your offerings you shall offer salt"
- LOGOS: "every grain offering of yours you shall season with salt. You shall not let the salt of the covenant of your God be missing from your grain offering; with all your offerings you shall offer salt"
- PNEUMA: "Season every grain offering you bring with salt. Never let the salt of the covenant of your God be missing from your grain offering — you shall offer salt with all your offerings"

SOPHIA's ruling: Three sub-issues. (1) Opening salt command: The Hebrew titmalach melach is a figura etymologica — "you shall salt with salt" — an emphatic repetition. EMET preserves this ("with salt you shall salt"). LOGOS renders "season with salt," which is idiomatic but loses the figura. PNEUMA's imperative "Season" shifts the register. SOPHIA adopts a rendering that preserves the emphatic feel without the awkwardness: "with salt you shall salt every grain offering of yours" — keeping the prepositional phrase fronted while following EMET's literal impulse. (2) "Lacking" vs "missing": tashbit (from sh-b-t, "to cease/remove/stop") means to cause to be absent. "Be missing" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is natural English; "be lacking" (EMET) is also acceptable but slightly more formal. SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA: "be missing." (3) "From upon your grain offering" vs. "from your grain offering": EMET preserves the Hebrew me'al ("from upon"), LOGOS and PNEUMA simplify. SOPHIA follows LOGOS.

---

**v.14 — The firstfruits grain construct: rendering of aviv qaluy ba-esh geres karmel**
- EMET: "roasted [qaluy] grain [aviv] crushed [karmel] you shall bring near as your grain offering of firstfruits"
- LOGOS: "you shall bring your firstfruits grain offering as fresh ears roasted with fire, crushed new grain"
- PNEUMA: "offer it as heads of grain roasted over fire, crushed from fresh grain"

SOPHIA's ruling: This is the most difficult lexical crux of the chapter. The Hebrew sequence is: aviv (fresh ears / young grain) — qaluy ba-esh (roasted in fire) — geres (crushed/dehusked) — karmel (a term for fresh new grain; LXX: νέα, "new"). (1) EMET follows the Hebrew word order but renders awkwardly in English. (2) LOGOS separates the four terms into two phrases: "fresh ears roasted with fire, crushed new grain" — this preserves all four elements but may read as two separate things rather than one description. (3) PNEUMA's "heads of grain roasted over fire, crushed from fresh grain" reads most naturally but takes some interpretive liberty with "from fresh grain" for karmel. SOPHIA adjudicates: the four terms describe one offering — young grain on the stalk, fire-roasted, then crushed. "Fresh heads of grain, roasted in fire, crushed" captures all three stages; "karmel" as a qualifier ("new/fresh grain") modifies the whole. SOPHIA adopts: "fresh heads of grain roasted in fire, crushed as new grain" — drawing from LOGOS's elements and PNEUMA's natural flow, while keeping all four components visible.

---

**v.16 — "From its crushed grain and from its oil" vs. "some of the crushed grain and some of the oil"**
- EMET: "shall burn from it its memorial portion — from its crushed grain and from its oil — together with all its frankincense; a fire offering to the LORD"
- LOGOS: "shall burn as its memorial portion some of the crushed grain and some of the oil along with all its frankincense, a fire offering to the LORD"
- PNEUMA: "shall burn as its memorial portion some of the crushed grain and some of the oil, along with all the frankincense — a fire-offering to the LORD"

SOPHIA's ruling: EMET's em-dash construction ("from it its memorial portion — from its crushed grain and from its oil —") mirrors the Hebrew appositive structure but reads stiffly. LOGOS and PNEUMA agree on "some of the crushed grain and some of the oil," which is cleaner. LOGOS's "all its frankincense" (with possessive) vs. PNEUMA's "all the frankincense" (with article): the Hebrew has kol levonata ("all its frankincense") with a suffix, so EMET/LOGOS's "all its frankincense" is more faithful. SOPHIA follows LOGOS throughout: "shall burn as its memorial portion some of the crushed grain and some of the oil along with all its frankincense, a fire offering to the LORD."
